answer:
Five works in the corpus carry sustained material on the soul's journey after death:

1. Manusmṛti (unknown-manusmrti-347b76), chapter 12 — the explicit doctrine of karmaphala and rebirth: 'a Brahmana ... is reborn after death as a Chandala' (Mn_11.24, p-3c0534) and the threefold transmigration (cited in Mn_1.116, p-8924d2 of ch.1).

2. Yājñavalkya Smṛti (unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6), chapter 3 — the Prāyaścittādhyāya, opens with rules of ritual impurity around death (āśaucaprakaraṇam, p-9dbce5) and proceeds to expiations that govern the soul's post-mortem fate.

3. San Hyan Kamahāyānikan (unknown-san-hyan-kamahayanikan-2a0c19), chapter 1 — Old Javanese Mahāyāna handbook on the path beyond death; the cognate text San Hyan Tattvajñāna (unknown-san-hyan-tattvajnana-1f29bd) ch.1 explicitly states that 'sattva, rajas, and tamas adhering to citta are the cause of the ātmā's transmigrations' (p-aea5b2).

4. Old English Elegies (unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e), chapter 10 — 'Soul and Body II' is the most concentrated Old English meditation on what happens after death: 'every living man / that he himself watch over his soul's journey, / how dreadful it will be when death descends' (p-34bcc7). The damned soul returns to scold its body every seven nights for three hundred winters.

5. Cynewulf, Juliana (cynewulf-juliana-9a2157) — the saint's life ends with judgment imagery; the broader Old English Christian frame ('after death seek out the Lord', cf. Andreas p-ba1f68) supplies the contrast against which the smṛti karma-cosmology can be read.
